schools.summary: The Philistines' test, whether the kine would bear the ark to Bethshemesh of themselves or it was chance: 'and see, if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Bethshemesh, then he hath done us this great evil: but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us' — the proof between God's hand and chance — 'and see, if it goeth up by the way of his own coast to Bethshemesh, then he hath done us this great evil: but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us': the Philistines devise a decisive test to learn whether God or chance had afflicted them (the witnesses noting the significance — the shrewd and decisive nature of the test, for the kine were untrained to the yoke, were nursing mothers parted from their calves, and by every law of nature would have turned back lowing to their young, if therefore against all nature they went straight up the road to Bethshemesh in Israel, drawing the ark, it could be nothing but the hand of God overruling them, and the Philistines would know certainly that their plagues were his stroke and no chance, the witnesses noting that the very heathen here reason rightly, that what is against nature and according to God's purpose is the proof of his hand, and that to ascribe such things to chance is the refuge of unbelief, the test that nature could not pass but God could overrule, the hand of God distinguished from chance, the heathen owning that God's overruling is the proof, the lesson — that nothing comes by chance, but the hand of God overrules all events, and the believer discerns his hand where the unbeliever sees only accident, 'the lot is cast into the lap; but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD,' Proverbs 16:33, the truth that not a sparrow falls without the Father, and all things are ordered by his providence, 'are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall on the ground without your Father,' Matthew 10:29, the figure — the overruling providence of God that governs all events, even the course of beasts, fulfilled in him who works all things after the counsel of his own will, 'who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will,' Ephesians 1:11, the truth that to call God's dealings chance is the blindness of unbelief, but faith sees his hand in all, the figure of the question whether the hand of God or mere chance rules in the events that befall men, the call to discern the hand of God in all that happens, the assurance that nothing befalls by chance but all by his providence); the Philistines testing whether the kine would go straight to Bethshemesh against nature, proving the hand of God (the test of God's hand, Proverbs 16:33) — the test (if it goeth to Bethshemesh); 'but if not, then we shall know that it is not his hand that smote us; it was a chance that happened to us' (the Philistines reasoning that if the kine turned back it was chance, but the untrained nursing cows going straight up against all nature could be nothing but God's overruling, not a sparrow falling without the Father, the hand of God against chance, Matthew 10:29; Ephesians 1:11) — the figure (the overruling providence of God that governs all events, the providence, the test, the kine going straight to Bethshemesh to follow, vv.10-12) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the test of the kine reveal about God's hand and chance?

sides.position: The question whether the hand of God or mere chance rules in the events that befall men - the untrained nursing kine going straight to Bethshemesh against all nature could be nothing but God's overruling, the heathen reasoning rightly that what is against nature is the proof of his hand, nothing coming by chance but God overruling all (Proverbs 16:33), not a sparrow falling without the Father (Matthew 10:29), God working all things after the counsel of his will (Ephesians 1:11)

the shrewd test, for the kine were untrained nursing mothers parted from their calves and by nature would have turned back, so that if against all nature they went straight to Bethshemesh it could be nothing but the hand of God overruling

schools.summary: The golden mice for all the cities, and the great stone a lasting witness: 'and the golden mice, according to the number of all the cities of the Philistines belonging to the five lords, both of fenced cities, and of country villages, even unto the great stone of Abel, whereon they set down the ark of the LORD: which stone remaineth unto this day in the field of Joshua, the Bethshemite' — the golden mice for all the cities, the stone a lasting witness (the and the golden mice, according to the number of all the cities of the Philistines belonging to the five lords, both of fenced cities, and of country villages, the golden mice, the even unto the great stone of Abel, whereon they set down the ark of the LORD: which stone remaineth unto this day in the field of Joshua, the Bethshemite, the great stone, the figure, the tribute for all the cities and the abiding memorial of the ark's return, the truth that the offering was for all the cities and the stone a witness, Joshua 4:9, the figure of the lasting memorial of God's mighty work, the golden mice, the great stone, vv.17); the golden mice for all the cities (the tribute, Joshua 4:9) — the mice (for all the cities); 'the great stone of Abel... which stone remaineth unto this day' (the stone a lasting witness of the return, the memorial, the figure, the lasting memorial of God's mighty work, the providence, the memorial, the smiting of the men who looked into the ark to follow, vv.19) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the golden mice, according to the number of all the cities of the Philistines belonging to the five lords, both of fenced cities, and of country villages, even unto the great stone of Abel, whereon they set down the ark of the LORD: which stone remaineth unto this day in the field of Joshua, the Bethshemite.
The golden mice for all the cities, and the great stone a lasting witness
the golden mice for all the cities and the great stone a lasting witness
the lasting memorial of God's mighty work (Joshua 4:9)

schools.summary: That God smote the men of Bethshemesh for looking into the ark: 'and he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD, even he smote of the people fifty thousand and threescore and ten men: and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter' — the holiness of God that demands reverence — 'and he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD... and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter': God struck the men of Bethshemesh dead for irreverently prying into the ark (the witnesses noting the significance — that the same God who had vindicated his honour against the heathen now vindicated it among his own people, for the men of Bethshemesh, in unholy curiosity, looked into the ark, which even the Levites might not do, and God smote them with a great slaughter, teaching that his holiness is not to be presumed upon, that familiarity must not breed irreverence, and that his own people may not profane his holy things any more than his enemies, the very mercy of the ark's return turned to judgment by their want of reverence, the witnesses noting that the judgment fell not because they rejoiced in the ark but because they treated the holy with irreverent boldness, the holiness that demands reverence from God's own people, the curiosity that profanes the holy, the mercy turned to judgment by irreverence, the lesson — that God is greatly to be feared and reverenced in the assembly of his saints, and his holy things not to be presumed upon, 'God is greatly to be feared in the assembly of the saints, and to be had in reverence of all them that are about him,' Psalm 89:7, the truth that to draw near to God irreverently is to provoke his judgment, as Nadab and Abihu, 'I will be sanctified in them that come nigh me,' Leviticus 10:3, the figure — the holiness of God that must be approached with reverence and godly fear, fulfilled in the worship that serves God acceptably, 'let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear: for our God is a consuming fire,' Hebrews 12:28-29, the truth that the nearer the privilege, the greater the reverence God requires, the figure of the holiness of God that will not be profaned even by his own people, and the reverence his presence demands, the call to draw near with reverence and godly fear, the warning against presuming upon the holy); God smiting the men of Bethshemesh for prying with unholy curiosity into the ark which even the Levites might not look into, the holiness of God vindicated among his own people (the judgment for irreverence, Psalm 89:7) — the smiting (looked into the ark); 'even he smote of the people fifty thousand and threescore and ten men: and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter' (the great slaughter and the people's lament, the mercy of the ark's return turned to judgment by their irreverence, God sanctified in them that come nigh him, the great slaughter, Leviticus 10:3; Hebrews 12:28-29) — the figure (the holiness of God approached with reverence and godly fear, the providence, the judgment, the men of Bethshemesh asking who can stand before this holy God to follow, vv.20) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why did God smite the men of Bethshemesh for looking into the ark?

sides.position: The holiness of God that will not be profaned even by his own people, and the reverence his presence demands - the men in unholy curiosity looking into the ark which even the Levites might not, his holiness not to be presumed upon, his own people not to profane his holy things, the mercy turned to judgment by irreverence, God greatly to be feared in the assembly of his saints (Psalm 89:7), sanctified in them that come nigh him (Leviticus 10:3), served with reverence for he is a consuming fire (Hebrews 12:28-29)

themes: And he smote the men of Bethshemesh, because they had looked into the ark of the LORD, even he smote of the people fifty thousand and threescore and ten men: and the people lamented, because the LORD had smitten many of the people with a great slaughter.
That God smote the men of Bethshemesh for looking into the ark (the crux of the holiness of God that will not be profaned even by his own people, and the reverence his presence demands)

the God who vindicated his honour against the heathen now vindicating it among his own people, the men of Bethshemesh in unholy curiosity looking into the ark which even the Levites might not, his holiness not to be presumed upon nor familiarity to breed irreverence

his own people not to profane his holy things, the mercy turned to judgment by their want of reverence, God greatly to be feared in the assembly of his saints, sanctified in them that come nigh him, served with reverence for he is a consuming fire (Psalm 89:7; Leviticus 10:3; Hebrews 12:28-29)

schools.summary: The men of Bethshemesh asking who could stand before the holy God: 'and the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us?' — the people, stricken, owned the holiness of God in awe (the and the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God?, the awe, the and to whom shall he go up from us?, the ark to be removed, the figure, the awe of the holy God that overwhelms the sinner, the sense of unworthiness, the truth that the people owned none could stand before the holy God, Malachi 3:2, the figure of the sinner's dread before the holiness of God, the awe of Bethshemesh, the holy God, vv.19); the people's awe before the holy God (the dread, Malachi 3:2) — the awe (who is able to stand); 'to whom shall he go up from us?' (the ark to be sent elsewhere, the sense of unworthiness, the figure, the sinner's dread before the holiness of God, the providence, the awe, the ark sent to Kirjathjearim to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the men of Bethshemesh said, Who is able to stand before this holy LORD God? and to whom shall he go up from us?
The men of Bethshemesh asking who could stand before the holy God
the people, stricken, owning the holiness of God in awe
the sinner's dread before the holiness of God (Malachi 3:2)

schools.summary: The men of Bethshemesh sending to Kirjathjearim to fetch the ark: 'and they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD; come ye down, and fetch it up to you' — the men of Bethshemesh sent the ark away to Kirjathjearim (the and they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD, the message, the come ye down, and fetch it up to you, fetch the ark, the figure, the ark passed on in fear, the holy presence both desired and dreaded, the truth that they sent the ark to Kirjathjearim after the smiting, 1 Samuel 7:1, the figure of the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep, the sending, the ark to Kirjathjearim, vv.20); the message sent to Kirjathjearim (the sending, 1 Samuel 7:1) — the message (the ark brought again); 'come ye down, and fetch it up to you' (the ark to be taken to Kirjathjearim, the removal, the figure, the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep, the providence, the sending, the ark abiding in Kirjathjearim and Samuel's reformation to follow, ch.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they sent messengers to the inhabitants of Kirjathjearim, saying, The Philistines have brought again the ark of the LORD; come ye down, and fetch it up to you.
The men of Bethshemesh sending to Kirjathjearim to fetch the ark
the men of Bethshemesh sending the ark to Kirjathjearim after the smiting
the holy presence that men both seek and fear to keep (1 Samuel 7:1)
